project_name: L42 — AutoEncoder Confusion: Latent Space Distortion Study
author: Hadar Wayn

execution_order_MANDATORY.rule: LOCAL RUNS FIRST — Colab notebook is built only after local succeeds
execution_order_MANDATORY.step_1_local: Run python main.py on local machine (UV virtual environment). All code must work and all results must be generated locally before touching Colab.
execution_order_MANDATORY.step_2_colab: Build the Colab notebook as a mirror of the local pipeline. The notebook replicates every phase, adds GPU acceleration, and displays results inline.
execution_order_MANDATORY.why: Running locally first means you understand every step before presenting it on Colab. The Colab notebook is the polished, GPU-powered showcase of what you already proved works on your own machine.

tasks.name: Create full directory structure
acceptance_criteria: All directories exist
All __init__.py files present
venv/.gitkeep contains UV setup instructions

tasks.name: Set up UV virtual environment and requirements.txt
acceptance_criteria: uv venv creates .venv successfully
uv pip install -r requirements.txt completes without errors
python -c 'import tensorflow, keras, numpy, matplotlib' exits with code 0
requirements.txt has exact pinned versions (e.g., tensorflow==2.16.1)

tasks.name: Implement ring buffer logging system
acceptance_criteria: logger.py is under 150 lines
RingBufferHandler rotates files at max_lines=1000
Deletes oldest log when max_files=5 is exceeded
setup_logger('l42') returns working logger

tasks.name: Implement relative path utilities
acceptance_criteria: get_project_root() returns correct path from any working directory
get_results_dir('experiment_a') creates directory if missing
NO hardcoded absolute paths anywhere in the file
Works on Windows WSL and Linux/Mac

tasks.name: Implement Fashion-MNIST loader (Experiment B)
acceptance_criteria: load_fashion_mnist() returns (x_sneakers_train, x_sneakers_val, x_boots_test)
x_sneakers_train contains ONLY class 7 (Sneaker) images — zero boots in training set
x_boots_test contains exactly 20 randomly selected class 9 (Ankle Boot) images
Random selection uses numpy.random.seed(42) for reproducibility
All pixel values normalized to [0, 1] range using NumPy vectorization (no loops)
Images reshaped to (N, 28, 28, 1) for Conv2D compatibility
Function prints: 'Training samples: X sneakers | Test samples: 20 ankle boots'

tasks.name: Implement Human Faces Dataset loader (Experiment A)
acceptance_criteria: load_human_faces() returns (x_male_train, x_male_val, x_female_test)
Supports TWO loading paths: (1) Kaggle API download, (2) local data/ folder fallback
x_female_test contains exactly 20 randomly selected female face images (seed=42)
x_male_train contains ONLY male faces — zero female faces in training set
All images resized to 64×64 RGB using PIL (NumPy batch resize, no Python loop)
Pixel values normalized to [0, 1]
If Kaggle download fails: print clear error message with manual download instructions
Function prints dataset statistics: count per gender, split sizes

tasks.name: Write data verification script
acceptance_criteria: verify_data() checks that training set contains ZERO contamination from test domain
Raises ValueError with clear message if contamination found
Prints sample counts and pixel value statistics (min, max, mean)

tasks.name: Build configurable Convolutional AutoEncoder with BatchNorm
acceptance_criteria: autoencoder.py is under 150 lines
build_autoencoder(config) returns dict: {'autoencoder': model, 'encoder': encoder_model, 'decoder': decoder_model}
Architecture matches class diagram: Input→Enc1(512)→Enc2(256)→Enc3(128)→Bottleneck→Dec1(128)→Dec2(256)→Dec3(512)→Output
Encoder uses Conv2D + BatchNormalization + ReLU + MaxPooling2D (downsampling)
Encoder ends with: Flatten → Dense(latent_dim) to produce latent vector
Decoder starts with: Dense(latent_dim) → Reshape, then UpSampling2D + Conv2DTranspose + BatchNormalization + ReLU
MANDATORY: BatchNormalization after EVERY Conv2D and Conv2DTranspose — prevents blurry output
Final decoder layer uses Sigmoid activation (outputs in [0,1])
Latent dimension: config['latent_dim'] — default 128 for faces, 64 for Fashion-MNIST
model.summary() prints correctly
Works for both 28×28 grayscale (Exp B) and 64×64 RGB (Exp A) via config['input_shape']
Encoder sub-model: input=full image, output=latent vector (Dense layer output)
Decoder sub-model: input=latent vector, output=reconstructed image
Adam optimizer with lr=0.001 (NOT SGD)
tasks.why_batchnorm: BatchNorm normalizes layer inputs, stabilizes gradients, prevents dead ReLU neurons. Without it, the network produces the muddy gray blurs seen in the reference L42 projects.

tasks.name: Validate architecture with dummy forward pass
acceptance_criteria: numpy.zeros((1, 64, 64, 3)) passes through autoencoder without shape error
numpy.zeros((1, 28, 28, 1)) passes through autoencoder without shape error
Output shape equals input shape for both experiments
Encoder output shape is (1, bottleneck_dim)

tasks.name: Implement training pipeline with checkpointing
acceptance_criteria: trainer.py is under 150 lines
train_autoencoder(autoencoder, x_train, x_val, config) returns history dict
Uses ModelCheckpoint: saves best weights to results/{experiment}/model_weights/best_weights.h5
Uses EarlyStopping with patience=10 (prevents overfitting)
Uses ReduceLROnPlateau for learning rate scheduling
History dict contains: {'train_loss': [...], 'val_loss': [...], 'epochs_run': N}
Prints progress: epoch number, train loss, val loss after each epoch
Training uses x_train as BOTH input AND target (autoencoder goal = reconstruct input)
Batch size is a config parameter (default 32, can be overridden for Colab GPU)

tasks.name: Explicit trained model export (SavedModel + H5 formats)
tasks.notes: Add save_model() call to trainer.py after training completes
acceptance_criteria: Experiment A: model saved to results/experiment_a/model_weights/autoencoder_final.h5
Experiment A: encoder sub-model saved to results/experiment_a/model_weights/encoder_final.h5
Experiment B: same structure under results/experiment_b/model_weights/
Saved models are loadable: keras.models.load_model(path) runs without error
Loading + inference produces identical output to training-time inference
README documents how to load and reuse the trained models
detect_hardware() returns dict: {'device': 'GPU/CPU', 'gpu_name': str, 'vram_gb': float, 'recommended_batch_size': int}
Batch size logic: CPU→32, T4/P4→64, P100/V100/A100→128
Prints hardware report table
Works in local environment (no Colab-specific imports)

tasks.name: Train Experiment B — Fashion-MNIST (Sneakers only)
acceptance_criteria: experiment_b_fashion.py is under 150 lines
Runs full pipeline: load→build→train→save
Final validation loss < 0.02 (BCE)
Training converges (loss decreases visibly over epochs — not flat from epoch 1)
Weights saved to results/experiment_b/model_weights/best_weights.h5
Training time < 10 minutes on local CPU

tasks.name: Train Experiment A — Human Faces (Male only)
acceptance_criteria: experiment_a_faces.py is under 150 lines
Runs full pipeline: load→build→train→save
Final validation loss < 0.05 (MSE)
Weights saved to results/experiment_a/model_weights/best_weights.h5
Training time < 30 minutes on local CPU (with early stopping)

tasks.name: Implement OoD inference pipeline
acceptance_criteria: confusion.py is under 150 lines
run_confusion(autoencoder, x_ood_test) returns dict with: {'originals', 'reconstructed', 'diff_maps', 'mse_per_image', 'mean_mse'}
diff_maps = numpy.abs(originals - reconstructed) — pure NumPy, no loops
mse_per_image computed via numpy.mean((originals - reconstructed)**2, axis=(1,2,3))
Also runs on 20 in-distribution samples for comparison baseline
Prints: 'OoD mean MSE: X.XXXX | In-distribution mean MSE: X.XXXX | Ratio: X.X×'

tasks.name: Verify confusion effect is visible
acceptance_criteria: OoD mean MSE is measurably HIGHER than in-distribution MSE (at least 1.5× ratio)
Visual inspection: at least 10 of 20 OoD reconstructions show visible structural changes
If confusion is NOT visible: investigate and fix (likely insufficient training or too-shallow architecture)

tasks.name: Plot 1: Convergence curves
acceptance_criteria: plot_convergence(history, output_path) saves PNG at 300 DPI
Shows training loss AND validation loss on same axes
X-axis: 'Epoch', Y-axis: 'Loss (MSE/BCE)'
Title: 'AutoEncoder Learning Curve — [Experiment Name]'
Subtitle: 'Loss decreasing = network is learning to reconstruct [domain] images'
Both experiments produce this plot

tasks.name: Plot 2: In-distribution reconstruction
acceptance_criteria: plot_in_distribution(originals, reconstructed, output_path) saves PNG
Shows 5 pairs: [Original | Reconstructed] side by side
Title: 'Network Works Correctly on Training Domain'
Subtitle: 'These are the images the network learned — it reconstructs them accurately'
MSE shown below each pair

tasks.name: Plot 3: OoD confusion grid (20 images)
acceptance_criteria: plot_confusion_grid(originals, reconstructed, diff_maps, mse_list, output_path) saves PNG
Grid layout: 20 rows × 3 columns = [Original | Reconstructed | Difference Map]
Difference map uses 'hot' colormap (bright = large distortion)
MSE value shown for each row
Title: 'The Confusion Effect — Network Hallucinates Training-Domain Features'
Column headers: 'Original Input (OoD)' | 'Reconstructed Output' | 'Distortion Map'
Figure size: (12, 50) for readability of all 20 images

tasks.name: Plot 4: Top-5 distortion highlights
acceptance_criteria: plot_distortion_highlights(originals, reconstructed, mse_list, experiment_name, output_path)
Shows top 5 images sorted by HIGHEST MSE (most confused)
Each image pair has annotation text explaining the specific distortion
Experiment A annotations: 'Jawline squared', 'Facial hair artifact', etc.
Experiment B annotations: 'Boot shaft chopped', 'Profile compressed', etc.
Title: 'Top 5 Most Confused Reconstructions'

tasks.name: Plot 5: Error distribution comparison
tasks.notes: Add to visualizer.py. visualizer.py total must stay under 150 lines — split if needed.
acceptance_criteria: plot_error_distribution(in_dist_mse, ood_mse, output_path) saves PNG
Overlapping histograms: in-distribution (blue) vs OoD (red)
Vertical dashed line at mean of each distribution
X-axis: 'Reconstruction MSE', Y-axis: 'Count'
Title: 'Reconstruction Error: Normal vs Confused'
Subtitle: 'OoD images have higher error — the network struggles with unfamiliar input'
Legend shows mean values

tasks.name: Plot 6 (Optional): PCA Latent Space Visualization
tasks.notes: Add to src/visualizer_latent.py (separate file to stay under 150-line limit)
acceptance_criteria: Encode all training images through the encoder sub-model to get latent vectors
Encode 20 OoD test images through the same encoder
Apply PCA (sklearn.decomposition.PCA, n_components=2) to reduce latent vectors to 2D
Scatter plot: in-distribution points = blue, OoD points = red stars
Title: 'Latent Space: Where OoD Images Land'
Subtitle: 'Red stars = OoD inputs forced into wrong region of the latent space'
Include convex hull or cluster ellipse around each group to show separation
Saved as results/experiment_X/latent_space_pca.png at 300 DPI
If PCA shows clear separation between blue and red clusters = strong proof of confusion effect
visualizer_latent.py is under 150 lines
tasks.educational_value: This is the geometric proof of why confusion happens. A visitor can see: the red dots (OoD) land in the wrong neighborhood of the latent map, so the decoder reconstructs them using the wrong blueprint.

tasks.name: Run all visualizations for both experiments
acceptance_criteria: results/experiment_a/ contains 5 PNG files
results/experiment_b/ contains 5 PNG files
All images open and display correctly
All PNG files are > 100KB (proving proper 300 DPI save)

tasks.name: Write main.py entry point
acceptance_criteria: main.py is under 150 lines
python main.py runs both experiments end-to-end
python main.py --experiment a runs only Experiment A
python main.py --experiment b runs only Experiment B
Prints clear section headers for each phase
Final output: 'All results saved to results/ directory'

phases.phase_title: Google Colab Notebook — Built AFTER Local Succeeds
phases.phase_note: ⚠️ DO NOT START THIS PHASE until all local phases (1–7) are complete and verified. The notebook is a mirror of the local pipeline — you can only mirror something that already works.

tasks.name: Create complete Colab notebook mirroring the local pipeline
tasks.prerequisite: Local main.py must run successfully end-to-end before this task begins
acceptance_criteria: Notebook is valid JSON (.ipynb format)
Cell 1 (Markdown): Course banner with 'Author: Hadar Wayn', project title, what this notebook demonstrates
Cell 2 (Code): GPU detection — runs !nvidia-smi, detects GPU type, recommends batch size
Cell 3 (Code): Package installation — !pip install all required packages
Cell 4 (Code): Google Drive mount — mounts drive, creates /content/drive/MyDrive/L42_results/ folder
Cell 5 (Markdown): Plain-English explanation of AutoEncoders with the same artist analogy from the README
Cells 6–10: Experiment B (Fashion-MNIST) — one cell per phase, same phase names as local pipeline
Cells 11–15: Experiment A (Human Faces) — same structure
Cell 16: Training time comparison table — Local CPU time vs Colab GPU time side by side
Cell 17: Final summary — display all result images inline with the same captions as the README
EVERY code cell is preceded by a Markdown cell that explains: what this cell does, why this step exists, what output to expect
All 6 visualizations per experiment displayed inline with plt.show()
Notebook structure matches local pipeline phase-for-phase — a reader can compare notebook cells directly to local Python files

tasks.name: Verify notebook runs top-to-bottom without errors
acceptance_criteria: All cells execute in sequence with no unhandled exceptions
GPU detection cell runs even on CPU-only runtime
Fashion-MNIST loads without manual download (built-in Keras)
Human Faces section: graceful error handling if Kaggle API not configured
All 10 result images display inline in the notebook
Notebook re-runs cleanly after Runtime > Restart and run all

Section: Applications & Use Cases (at least 3 scenarios)
Section: How AutoEncoders Work (diagram-style markdown table + step-by-step)
Section: UV Virtual Environment Setup (full instructions for WSL and PowerShell)

Section: How to Run (local + Colab link)
Section: Code Files Summary (table: filename | description | lines) — all files under 150 lines
Section: Results — embeds ALL 10 PNG visualizations with plain-English explanations
Section: What I Learned (3+ technical insights + 3+ real-world connections)
Section: GitHub upload instructions
All embedded images use relative paths: results/experiment_a/...
README is engaging — a recruiter can understand the project in 2 minutes

tasks.name: Fresh environment test (local)
acceptance_criteria: Delete .venv, recreate with 'uv venv', install with 'uv pip install -r requirements.txt'
python main.py --experiment b completes successfully
results/experiment_b/ contains 5 PNG files
No 'module not found' errors

tasks.name: Code quality audit
acceptance_criteria: ALL Python files are under 150 lines (verify with: wc -l src/*.py)
No hardcoded absolute paths anywhere
No raw Python loops over numpy arrays
All functions have type hints and docstrings
No secrets or API keys in committed code

tasks.name: Visual QA — confusion effect verification
acceptance_criteria: Experiment B (Boots→Sneakers): at least 15 of 20 boots show upper shaft compression
Experiment A (Female→Male model): at least 10 of 20 female faces show visible structural changes
Confusion grid PNG is clear and not blurry (300 DPI confirmed)
OoD MSE is at least 1.5× higher than in-distribution MSE for both experiments

tasks.name: Git repository setup and GitHub push
acceptance_criteria: git init, git add ., git commit -m 'Initial commit: L42 AutoEncoder Confusion'
.gitignore excludes: .venv/, __pycache__/, *.pyc, .env, logs/*.log
venv/.gitkeep IS committed
results/ PNG files ARE committed (they are the deliverable)
model_weights/ may be excluded if files are too large (>100MB)
git push -u origin main succeeds
README displays correctly on GitHub with all images visible

risk_1_blurry_output.symptoms: Reconstructed images are smeared gray blobs with no visible structure
root_causes: Flat loss curve = network not learning
mitigations: BatchNormalization after EVERY Conv2D layer (mandatory in task 3.1)
Latent dim must be 64-128, not 8 or 16
Verify loss decreases — if flat from epoch 1, reduce learning rate

risk_2_no_visible_confusion.symptoms: OoD images reconstruct correctly — no distortion visible
root_causes: Training set contaminated with OoD images
mitigations: Verify training set purity (task 2.3 data verification)
Reduce latent_dim to force stronger compression bias
Ensure both domains normalized identically to [0,1]
Double-check Fashion-MNIST label filtering: 7=Sneaker only in train, 9=Boot only in test

risk_3_kaggle_auth_failure.mitigation: Graceful fallback to local data/ folder — instructions in README

risk_4_colab_oom.mitigation: GPU detection auto-adjusts batch size; fallback: reduce face image size to 32x32

extensions_optional.name: Variational AutoEncoder (VAE)
extensions_optional.description: Replace standard AE with VAE — adds KL divergence loss, creates continuous smooth latent space

extensions_optional.name: Latent Space Interpolation
extensions_optional.description: Interpolate between male and female latent vectors, decode each step → morphing animation

extensions_optional.name: Cross-Domain Encoder-Decoder Swapping
extensions_optional.description: Train two separate autoencoders, swap decoders to force cross-domain reconstruction

extensions_optional.name: Anomaly Detection Demo
extensions_optional.description: Apply confusion principle to medical imaging: train on healthy X-rays, flag pneumonia by reconstruction error

success_definition.primary: Person with no AI knowledge sees confusion grids and immediately understands: 'The network is trying to turn boots into sneakers'
success_definition.portfolio_standard: README + results are impressive enough to include in a job application portfolio
